title: Scouring and mordanting silk for natural dyeing
subtitle: How to remove sericin from raw silk and apply an alum mordant before natural dyeing
excerpt: Silk must be scoured to remove its natural sericin coating before natural dyeing; undiscoured silk takes dye unevenly and the sericin degrades over time with washing. Once scoured, silk is mordanted with alum in a gentle warm bath before entering the dye. Silk is more delicate than wool and requires lower temperatures and more careful handling throughout.
sourceNotes: Silk scouring and mordanting is documented in Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour', Sasha Duerr's work, and in specialist silk dyeing instruction. The temperature and handling constraints described are consistent across all sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of washing natural fibre to remove oils, processing agents, or (in silk) the sericin gum before dyeing. Silk scouring removes the sticky outer coating to reveal the smooth, lustrous filament beneath. Wool scouring removes lanolin and vegetable matter.

The natural protein gum that bonds together the two strands of raw silk filament. Sericin makes raw silk stiff and reduces dye uptake; removing it (scouring) reveals the smooth, naturally lustrous silk. Some sericin is intentionally left in 'raw' or 'noil' silk, giving it a slightly matte, textured quality.

Potassium aluminium sulphate, the most widely used mordant for natural dyeing. Applied at 10 to 15 percent of the dry weight of fibre, alum forms a molecular bond between the dye molecule and the fibre protein, improving colour depth and washfastness. It is safe at dye quantities and is the preferred mordant for silk, which is sensitive to stronger mordants.

content.text: Raw or scoured-but-unmordanted silk takes natural dye unevenly. The colour washes out more readily than on properly prepared silk.

prepares the fibre to accept dye fully. Both steps must be done gently. Silk is permanently damaged by high temperatures, agitation, and alkalis. The Garden category covers growing dye plants that pair well with silk, particularly Japanese indigo and weld.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Alum is safe at dye quantities. Keep all dye equipment separate from food preparation. Never use alkali soaps on silk.

items.name: Raw or thrown silk: fabric, yarn, or fibre, dry weight
pH-neutral liquid soap or specialist silk scouring agent
Water, soft or softened if possible
Large stainless steel dye pot, at least 5 litres

content.text: Fill the dye pot with 3 litres of water and heat to 60°C. Add the pH-neutral liquid soap and stir gently. Do not use washing-up liquid or laundry detergent: both may be alkaline enough to damage silk. Enter the silk gently, handling as little as possible. Silk in hot water is fragile and can crease or stretch permanently under its own weight; support the fibre fully when moving it.

Hold the bath at 60°C for 30 minutes. Move the silk very gently every 10 minutes. Do not agitate. The water will become cloudy as the sericin dissolves into solution. After 30 minutes, remove the silk carefully and rinse in water at the same temperature. Avoid temperature shock: moving silk directly from hot to cold water causes the fibres to contract suddenly and can cause permanent creasing in silk fabric.

Dissolve 12 g of alum in 3 litres of warm water (50°C). Enter the still-damp scoured silk and heat gradually to 65°C over 20 minutes. Hold at 65°C for 45 minutes, moving gently every 10 minutes. Silk requires a lower mordanting temperature than wool (65°C rather than 80°C); higher temperatures can dull the natural lustre of the silk. After mordanting, remove the silk and proceed directly to the dye bath without rinsing: the alum must remain on the fibre.

items.problem: The silk feels stiff and gummy after scouring rather than smooth
items.cause: The sericin was not fully dissolved, either because the temperature was too low or the scouring time was too short
items.fix: Return the silk to a fresh scouring bath at 60°C with a small amount of soap and hold for a further 20 minutes. Very heavily sericined raw silk (such as unscoured Thai silk) may need two scouring sessions to fully remove the gum.

items.problem: The silk loses its lustre after mordanting and appears dull
items.cause: The mordant bath temperature exceeded 70°C, which can affect the structure of the silk protein
items.fix: Keep the mordant bath strictly below 70°C. If lustre is important, reduce the mordanting temperature to 60°C and extend the bath duration to 60 minutes to compensate.
